problem_summary: 2010 MCM Problem B (discrete): geographic profiling of a serial killer. Given a sparse set of crime-site locations and their times, the team must (a) build a scheme to locate the killer's likely residence/anchor point and (b) predict where the next crime is likely to occur. The defining constraint is data scarcity — far fewer points than for burglary/auto-theft profiling — so methods must extract signal from a handful of geo-temporal events.
models_used: Centrographic model (Model 1): treats crime sites as point masses and tracks the spatial-mean 'centroid' C_j = (1/j)Sum X_Ki over time, building a kinematic analogy — centroidal velocity V_C, mass = number of events j, and linear momentum P_C = j*V_C.

Conservation-of-momentum update for the centroid: each new kill K_j is modeled as a perfectly inelastic collision with the existing centroid, giving the recursive update V_Cj = [(j-1)V_C(j-1) + V_Kj]/j, with single-event velocity V_Kj = (X_Kj - C_(j-1))/(t_j - t_(j-1)).

Time-dependent prediction region A(t): a polygon centered at Center_A(t) = C_j + f(t)V_Cj + g(t)*a_hat_j, where a_hat_j is a regression-estimated centroidal acceleration and f(t)=2t/(1+e^{βt}), g(t)=2t^2/(1+e^{βt})^2 are damping coefficients (chosen so the kinematic terms match t and t^2/2 for short times but the center stops drifting as t→∞).
Linear regression on centroidal velocity (longitudinal and latitudinal independently) to estimate the acceleration term a_hat, reported with R^2 and p-values.

Rational Serial Criminal (RSC) model (Model 2): a microeconomic revealed-preference / rational-choice model. Killer is a rational agent choosing a probability vector p over n grid cells (analogue of a consumption bundle) to maximize a utility U(p,c) subject to Sum p_i = 1; 'price' c_i = perceived incremental risk of capture in cell i.

Concrete functional forms for RSC: cost c(X_j) = 1 if cell already used else 0 ('never return to scene of a perfect crime'), and taste/likelihood k_i(c,α) = 0 if i=α, else (1-c_i)/(d(α,i)^k * normalizer) — a distance-decay-from-anchor-point with buffer zone.

Bayesian inversion for the anchor point: with a uniform prior P(α), Bayes gives P(α=i | K) ∝ P(K | α=i), where P(K|α)=Prod_j k_Kj(c(X_(j-1)),α); plotting this yields a 'jeopardy surface' (isopleth map) ranking residence likelihood per cell.
Next-strike prediction via marginalization over anchor points: p_hat_i ∝ Sum_j Sum_α k_i(c(X_(j-1)),α) P(K_1..K_(j-1)|α).
Manhattan (L1) distance metric for error/travel distance, justified as a better model of urban travel than Euclidean.

Hit Score % evaluation metric (borrowed from Rossmo's CGT): fraction of the hunting-area grid that must be searched, following the jeopardy surface from high to low, before reaching the true home; multiplied by hunting area to give 'effective search area'.
Chi-square goodness-of-fit test comparing the achieved Hit Score % against a uniform-search baseline to show the RSC model beats random search at the 95% level.
key_techniques: Cross-domain analogy as the core idea generator: physics (center of mass, momentum, inelastic collision, velocity/acceleration/jerk) for Model 1, and microeconomics (revealed preference, utility maximization, budget/price, mixed strategies) for Model 2 — two genuinely different lenses on the same data.

Encoding the temporal dimension that a plain spatial mean throws away: using inter-event times to give the centroid velocity and momentum, so the prediction evolves with time even when no new kill has occurred.

Bayesian inversion with a uniform prior to turn a forward generative model P(crimes | anchor) into a backward 'where does he live' jeopardy surface, sidestepping the need for any normalization constant (only ranking matters).
Designing the RSC to be agnostic to the utility/taste function k — making the framework a plug-in skeleton police can extend with their own beliefs (zoning, demographics, victim activity spaces).

Validation on three real, named serial killers (Berkowitz/Son of Sam, Buono-Bianchi/Hillside Strangler, Muhammad/DC Sniper) with data hand-built from Wikipedia + period newspaper articles and geocoded addresses to lat/long.

Honest, adversarial self-testing: deliberately reporting the case where the model fails (Berkowitz's 63.2% Hit Score, worse than random) and diagnosing it (he moved residence mid-spree), then showing the 18.1% result on his prior home.
Borrowing a domain-standard, interpretable accuracy metric (Hit Score %) instead of inventing one, plus statistical-significance backing (R^2, p-values, chi-square) for claims.
Sequential leave-future-out testing: predict the j-th kill from the first j-1, sweeping j to show error distance shrinking as data accumulate (mean ~15 km across all of LA).

Showing the two models compose: a coarse-to-fine idea where the centrographic prediction narrows the window so the expensive RSC grid can use variable resolution, and RSC probabilities can damp the centroid's motion.
why_it_won:
"It delivered exactly what a discrete-problem judge wants under data scarcity: two independent, theoretically grounded models (not one model with a parameter swept), each rooted in a respectable literature (Rossmo, LeBeau, Samuelson, Becker) and each producing a concrete, actionable police deliverable — a search region and a jeopardy heatmap. The team did the unglamorous work most teams skip: they built real datasets from three historical killers, geocoded them, ran code, and validated against ground truth with a recognized metric (Hit Score %) plus genuine statistics. Crucially, they were intellectually honest — they surfaced their own worst failure (Berkowitz, worse-than-random because he relocated) and explained it rather than hiding it, which reads as scientific maturity to judges. The microeconomic framing of a criminal as a rational utility-maximizer over a probability vector is novel and memorable, and the physics-collision derivation of the centroid update is elegant. The writing is confident and even witty while staying rigorous, and the executive summary is genuinely operational — it tells a police chief how many crime sites to supply, what assumptions hold, and when the tool breaks. Strong idea-novelty + real validation + honest limitations + actionable output is the classic O-award signature."
"
section_highlights: "Structure follows the canonical MCM arc: restatement/teaser opening → Abstract → Introduction (roadmap of sections) → Background/literature → Models (Centrographic, then RSC, each split Background/Assumptions/Model or Theory/Practice) → Data → Results (per-model, then Combined) → Discussion → Executive Summary → References. Standout sections: (1) The Models section, which separates 'Theory' (general derivation, any serial criminal) from 'Practice' (the specific serial-killer implementation with explicit assumptions) — a clean abstraction boundary. (2) The Discussion, which is unusually self-critical: it interrogates whether the linear acceleration regression is even valid (good R^2/p in latitude, statistically insignificant in longitude) and questions its own f(t)/g(t) choices and β-sensitivity. (3) The Executive Summary, which is written for a non-mathematical police audience and gives operational rules of thumb (use ≥5 crime sites, only accurately-known sites, collapse kill-site vs body-dump duplicates, β = t̄/ln15). Assumptions are stated up front per model and re-stated for the practitioner. Figures pair the data points with the predicted diamond/heatmap and the true location, making accuracy visually legible. Notable writing texture: footnotes with dry humor ('But nor do we deny it') and a literary reference (Concrete Blonde lyric) cited for the 'never return to the scene' assumption — confident voice without sacrificing rigor."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Centrographic model is highly sensitive to the hand-tuned parameter β, which has no principled derivation and must be re-fit per killer (the team admits this is the model's main weakness).

The kinematic acceleration term a_hat rests on a linear regression of velocity that is statistically insignificant in the longitude direction (low R^2, high p in Table 3) — so a core component of the model is not actually supported by the data the team presents.
The f(t) and g(t) damping functions are chosen ad hoc to satisfy three constraints; the authors concede the specific choice may be ill-advised and untested across datasets.
Tiny-N statistics throughout: predictions and regressions are built on 4-9 points; the chi-square 'beats random' claim aggregates only three Hit Score results.

Strong, sometimes violated assumptions: stationary anchor point (broken by Berkowitz's move, producing the 63.2% worse-than-random result), uniform spatial prior (half the Berkowitz hunting area was literally underwater), and no two crimes in the same cell (broken by the DC Sniper data).
No geography/population baked in — water, terrain, road network, and population density are all ignored; the team offloads this to 'police will fill in the gaps.'
Temporal degeneracy: same-day kills at different times produce near-infinite centroidal velocity, making the DC Sniper case partly nonsensical for Model 1.
Validation is in-sample/retrospective on famous solved cases the team curated; no out-of-sample or blind test, and crime-site selection is admittedly subjective.
Predictions can fall entirely off the map (Berkowitz's final Brooklyn kill, the DC Sniper's southward turn) — the models have no mechanism for regime changes or long jumps.

RSC's k-function is a simple inverse-distance-power with a single home; it cannot represent multi-anchor, commuter, or victim-driven (red-light-district) offenders, which the team flags as out of scope (e.g., the Gacy case where all sites coincide).
reusable_patterns: Two-lens strategy: attack one prediction target with two methodologically independent models from different disciplines (a physical/kinematic one and a decision-theoretic/economic one), then show they can be composed — this signals depth and robustness to judges far better than one tuned model.

Turn a static summary statistic into a dynamic predictor by attaching a time-derivative: take any 'center/mean' the problem suggests and give it velocity/momentum from inter-event times so the forecast evolves between observations.
Physics-collision bookkeeping for online updates: model adding the n-th data point as an inelastic collision to get a clean recursive mean/centroid update with a natural 1/n weighting.

Bayes-with-uniform-prior to invert a forward generative model into an inverse 'where is the source' likelihood surface, and explicitly drop normalization when you only need a ranking — cheaper and cleaner.

Build the model as a skeleton parameterized by a domain-supplied function (here utility/taste k), so the framework generalizes and the end-user (police) can inject their own beliefs — a strong 'extensibility' selling point.
Adopt the target field's own evaluation metric (Hit Score % / effective search area) rather than inventing one, and back every quantitative claim with a significance test (R^2, p, chi-square).

Construct real validation datasets from public sources (Wikipedia + newspapers, geocoded to lat/long) and test by leave-future-out: predict event j from events 1..j-1 and show error shrinking as data accumulate.
Report your model's worst failure case and diagnose its cause (assumption violated) — controlled honesty builds judge trust more than a flawless-looking but cherry-picked results section.

Write a genuinely operational executive summary: concrete usage rules (minimum data points, what to include/exclude, when the tool does NOT apply, an example parameter value) aimed at the non-technical decision-maker named in the prompt.
Choose and justify the metric to match the domain (Manhattan over Euclidean for urban travel distance) instead of defaulting to Euclidean — small, defensible modeling choices accumulate credibility.
